title: Chapter 16 parent inline-cue page render
desc: Parent page render for Chapter 16 showing the LBNL/DOE paragraph with through inline before comparison with the N16-1 sidenote candidate.
text: Parent inline-cue render for. Candidate comparison uses.

g.text: By the middle of the 2020s, the frontier race had already taught its participants
to think in tokens, GPUs, dense racks, networking fabrics, model-parallel training
runs, inference latency, and capital expenditure. NVIDIA could stand on a keynote
stage and sell the "AI factory" as the next industrial unit. That was a company
argument, not a neutral facility fact. [ inline]
This was not simply the old complaint that computers used electricity. The IEA
estimated data centres at roughly 1.5 percent of global electricity consumption
in 2024 [ inline], with a base case where data-centre electricity more than
doubled by 2030. [ inline]
The United States made the compression visible. Lawrence Berkeley National
Laboratory and the Department of Energy reported about 176 terawatt-hours of
U.S. data-centre electricity use in 2023, then modeled 2028 scenarios ranging
from 325 to 580 terawatt-hours. [; ]
In share terms, the report put U.S. data centres at about 4.4 percent of electricity
use in 2023 and projected a possible 6.7 to 12 percent by 2028.
[; ] Those were scenarios, not prophecy.
That mismatch is the spine of this chapter: speed to power. The LLM race is
usually narrated as a race for intelligence, chips, talent, capital, and data.
By the time models became products, it had also become a race for the notes
to plug in.
Cooling was not a plumbing footnote. Uptime's operator survey added a caution:
average PUE had changed little for a sixth consecutive year, and high rack density
remained uncommon. [; inline]
The stack is only as fast as the slowest layer that matters at that moment. It is
not yet a quantified energy-per-token model. [ inline]

text: Inline cues preserve exact row mapping but create two dense source clusters in the LBNL/DOE paragraph.
